fact: January 26 is exactly 26 days into the year - the date matches the number of days that have passed!
In Antarctica, this is peak summer and the sun never sets, creating a weird orange glow that lasts all day and night.
Polar bears in the Arctic are having babies right now - tiny cubs the size of hamsters are being born in cozy snow dens.
In many northern places, January 26 typically hits some of the coldest temperatures of the entire winter - cold enough to freeze your boogers instantly outside!
Maple trees in cold regions are secretly making sugar right now - sap starts flowing upward even though it's still freezing outside.
The sun rises about 2 minutes earlier than it did just a week ago in most places, but you probably can't even notice the difference yet.
January 26 falls in the middle of Aquarius season, when the 'water bearer' constellation is easiest to spot in the night sky.
Penguins in the Southern Hemisphere are often molting right now - they look like walking feather explosions and can't swim until new feathers grow in.
Desert foxes grow their fluffiest winter coats around this time, making their ears look comically oversized compared to summer.
The moon phase on January 26 changes every year, but when it's a new moon, you can see way more stars because there's zero moonlight pollution.
Groundhogs are still fast asleep underground and won't wake up for another week or two - they're basically furry hibernating potatoes right now.
In tropical places, January 26 typically marks the dry season when rivers shrink and animals crowd around the few remaining water holes like a nature traffic jam.
